reasoning_model_1: The model generates the text 'Oh my god, I won the lottery. It is incredible.' The script requested 'It's', but the model expanded it to 'It is', which is a minor content deviation. In terms of the core dimension 'Pitch', the user requested a 'very high pitch'. The audio provided (00:00-00:03) features a standard female voice with a normal, average pitch range. There is no audible attempt to raise the pitch to a 'very high' level as requested. The tone is somewhat flat and lacks the excitement associated with winning a lottery, sounding more like a calm statement. Consequently, it fails to meet the specific paralinguistic constraint.
reasoning_model_2: The model generates the text 'Oh my god, I won the lottery! It is incredible!' Similar to Model 1, it expands 'It's' to 'It is'. Regarding the core dimension 'Pitch', the model successfully alters the voice to a very high register (00:00-00:04). The voice sounds like a falsetto or significantly pitch-shifted character, distinctly different from a normal speaking voice. This aligns perfectly with the instruction for 'very high pitch'. The delivery also conveys high energy and excitement, matching the context of the sentence, although the primary evaluation focus here is the successful application of the high pitch feature.
model_comparison: Model 2 is the clear winner. The user specifically requested the sentence be read with a 'very high pitch'. Model 1 completely ignored this constraint, delivering the line in a standard, normal-pitch female voice with flat affect. Model 2, on the other hand, generated audio with a distinctively high pitch (falsetto/cartoon-like), effectively adhering to the user's specific paralinguistic command. Both models had a minor text expansion ('It's' to 'It is'), but only Model 2 satisfied the core feature requirement.
